coredata.dc:title: Surface modification of carbon quantum dots by fluorescein derivative for dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent hypochlorite biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fanyong
Bai, Zhangjun
Ma, Tengchuang
Sun, Xiaodong
Zu, Fanlin
Luo, Yunmei
Chen, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Surface modification of carbon quantum dots by fluorescein (FH-GA-CQDs) were synthesized for dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent ClO− biosensing and in vivo bioimaging. In the presence of ClO−, FH-GA-CQDs solution shows gradually decrease in the emission intensity at 413 nm and rapidly increase in the emission at 508 nm, corresponding to the blue-to-green emission color change. The detection mechanism is that the ClO− induces the ring-opening of FH units to form an oxadiazole structure, resulting in energy transferring from CQDs moiety to FH units. The energy transfer efficiency and energy transfer rate were estimated as 0.34 and 4.00 × 10−7 s−1, respectively. The results reveal that the FH-GA-CQDs have high sensitivity and selectivity for ClO− in the linear range extending from 0 to 70 μM with a detection limit as low as 93 nM. Meanwhile, the potency of the FH-GA-CQDs detecting ClO− in real water examples were validated by recoveries from 94% to 106% and semi-quantification determination of ClO− on fluorescent test strips. Most importantly, FH-GA-CQDs display low cytotoxicity and were successfully applied for sensing of ClO− in living cells and imaging of ClO− in nude mice.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Surface modification
Dual-emission
Hypochlorite
Biosensing
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection of ClO− based on FH-GA-CQDs
Fluorescence detection of ClO− using FH-GA-CQDs
Detection mechanism of FH-GA-CQDs for ClO−
Detection of ClO− in real water with the FH-GA-CQDs
Biosensing of ClO− in living cells and imaging of nude mice with the FH-GA-CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology of China
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) CQDs, (d) GA-CQDs and (g) FH-GA-CQDs; HRTEM images of (b) CQDs, (e) GA-CQDs and (h) FH-GA-CQDs; Size distribution histograms of (c) CQDs, (f) GA-CQDs and (i) FH-GA-CQDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of CQDs, GA-CQDs, FH, FH-GA-CQDs; (b) XPS analysis of CQDs, GA-CQDs, FH-GA-CQDs; (c) Zeta potential of CQDs, GA-CQDs, FH, FH-GA-CQDs.

(a) UV–vis spectra of CQDs, GA-CQDs, FH, FH-GA-CQDs and FH-GA-CQDs + ClO− in Tris buffer solution (pH 7.4, 10 mM); (b) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs, GA-CQDs, FH (lactam ring opened by alkali treatment), FH-GA-CQDs and FH-GA-CQDs + ClO− in Tris buffer solution (pH 7.4, 10 mM); (c) Fluorescence spectra of GA-CQDs (λex = 330 nm, a: red) and UV–vis spectra of the FH (lactam ring opened by alkali treatment (b: green) in Tris buffer solution (pH 7.4, 10 mM) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) Fluorescence response of FH-GA-CQDs to 50 μM various ROS/RNS under excited at 330 nm in Tris buffer solution (pH 7.4, 10 mM); (b) UV–vis absorption response of FH-GA-CQDs to various ROS/RNS in Tris buffer solution (pH 7.4, 10 mM); (c) The color photographs of FH-GA-CQDs to various ROS/RNS and fluorescence photographs of FH-GA-CQDs to various ROS/RNS under excited at 365 nm in Tris buffer solution (pH 7.4, 10 mM).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of FH-GA-CQDs with addition of different amounts of ClO− under excited at 330 nm in Tris buffer solution (pH 7.4, 10 mM); (b) Evolution of CIE chromaticity coordinates of FH-GA-CQDs for the response of ClO− at different concentrations derived from fluorescence spectra; (c) The color photographs and fluorescence photographs (λex =365 nm) response of FH-GA-CQDs with addition of different amounts of ClO− in Tris buffer solution (pH 7.4, 10 mM).
Reaction time (a) and pH (b) influence on the fluorescence intensity of FH-GA-CQDs in the absence or presence of ClO− under excited at 330 nm.

(a) FT-IR spectra of the FH-GA-CQDs before and after addition of ClO−; (b) Fluorescence lifetime decay of the FH-GA-CQDs before and after addition of ClO−; (c) Schematic illustration of FH-GA-CQDs as FRET probes for the detection of ClO−.
Visual detection of ClO− in (a) tap water and (b) pool water. The photos were taken under a 365 nm UV lamp.

Fluorescence microscope images of VSMCs stained with 0.5 mg/mL of FH-GA-CQDs for 4 h (a); incubted with 50 μM ClO− for 1 h (b); and incubted with 50 μM ClO− for 2 h (c). Fluorescence microscope images of H1299 cells stained with 0.5 mg/mL of FH-GA-CQDs for 4 h (d); incubted with 50 μM ClO− for 2 h (b); and incubted with 100 μM ClO− for 2 h (c). The excitation wavelength of 395 nm.

Fluorescence imaging of the nude mice stained with 0.5 mg/mL of FH-GA-CQDs for 0.5 h in the absence and presence of 100 μM ClO− for 1 h and 2 h. The excitation wavelength of 380 nm, collecting fluorescence intensity range: 460–630 nm.
Comparison of different fluorescence probe for ClO− detection.

row.entry: real water, VMSCs, H1299 cells, nude mice

head.ce:title: Surface modification of carbon quantum dots by fluorescein derivative for dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent hypochlorite biosensing and in vivo bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, Tianjin Engineering Center for Safety Evaluation of Water Quality & Safeguards Technology, College of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin, 300387, China

Tianjin Engineering Center for Safety Evaluation of Water Quality & Safeguards Technology
College of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nuclear Medicine, Harbin Medical University Cancer Hospital, Harbin, Heilongjiang, 150081, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Basic Pharmacology of Ministry of Education and Joint International Research Loboratory of Ethnomedicine of Ministry of Education, Zunyi Medical University, Zunyi, 563003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Basic Pharmacology of Ministry of Education and Joint International Research Loboratory of Ethnomedicine of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Life Science, Hokkaido University, Sapporo, 0010024, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface Modification of Carbon Quantum Dots by Fluorescein Derivative for Dual-Emission Ratiometric Fluorescent Hypochlorite Biosensing and in Vivo Bioimaging.

Surface modification of carbon quantum dots by fluorescein (FH-GA-CQDs) were synthesized for dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent ClO− biosensing and in vivo bioimaging. In the presence of ClO−, FH-GA-CQDs solution shows gradually decrease in the emission intensity at 413 nm and rapidly increase in the emission at 508 nm, corresponding to the blue-to-green emission color change. The detection mechanism is that the ClO− induces the ring-opening of FH units to form an oxadiazole structure, resulting in energy transferring from CQDs moiety to FH units. The energy transfer efficiency and energy transfer rate were estimated as 0.34 and 4.00 × 10−7 s−1, respectively. The results reveal that the FH-GA-CQDs have high sensitivity and selectivity for ClO− in the linear range extending from 0 to 70 μM with a detection limit as low as 93 nM. Meanwhile, the potency of the FH-GA-CQDs detecting ClO− in real water examples were validated by recoveries from 94% to 106% and semi-quantification determination of ClO− on fluorescent test strips. Most importantly, FH-GA-CQDs display low cytotoxicity and were successfully applied for sensing of ClO− in living cells and imaging of ClO− in nude mice.

ce:section.ce:para: Hypochlorite (ClO−) as an efficient and safe oxidizer, has been widely used as bleach, deodorant and disinfectant in water purification [1]. The World Health Organization proposed that the minimum residual concentration of free chlorine in drinking water should be 0.2 mg L−1 [2]. A small amount of ClO− cannot sanitize certain bacteria or microbes to ensure the safety of supplying water, and a large amount of ClO− may produce undesirable byproducts such as trihalomethane (THMs), dichloroacetonitrile (DCAN), which is harmful to human beings and animals [3]. In addition, ClO−, as one of the most important reactive oxygen species, plays a crucial role in signal transductions of living organisms [4–6]. Endogenous ClO– produced by immune cells can act as powerful “guarder” in protecting the body against invasion of pathogens [7]. Abnormal ClO− levels can lead to inflammation-related diseases such as atherosclerosis, arthritis and cancer [8–11]. As a result, the development of a simple and convenient method to monitor ClO− in real water and biological samples is ever important.

Fluorescent probes, as a portability, easy operation and low-cost analytical tool, have been widely developed for the detection field [12]. Especially, some chromophores or their derivatives were synthesized for detecting ClO−, such as fluorescein, rhodamine, BODIPY and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [13–18]. The design principles of these probes are mainly based on the strong oxidation property of ClO−. Meanwhile, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as one of the most significant classes of fluorescent nanomaterials were applied in the fields of detecting analytes, bioimaging and drug delivery [19,20]. As the best candidates, CQDs are promising for replacing organic dye and QDs because of their low cytoxicity, good stability, easy preparation, and environmental friendliness [21–23]. By virtue of these outstanding properties, Simões et al. synthesized CQDs with citric acid, urea and sodium phosphate for the detection of ClO− [24]. Yin et al. prepared fluorescent CQDs with up- and down-conversion fluorescence for the dual-channel determination of ClO− [25]. However, the most pristine CQDs that contain oxygen and nitrogen functionalities are easily coordinated with metal ions, resulting poor selectivity for specific identification of ClO−. Meanwhile, the current CQDs-based probes response to analyte through quenching the fluorescence intensity, which is easily disturbed by a variety of factors, such as probe concentration, instrument efficiency and measurement conditions [26]. In contrast, ratiometric probes can avoid these problems and offer reliable detection method for analytes [27]. Therefore, it is very necessary to design the ratiometric probe by grafting organic fluorescence fluorophore on CQDs for special recognition of ClO−.

Very recently, our group reported three highly sensitive and selective fluorescent probes based on organic molecule modified-CQDs for the detection of analytes [28–30]. To broaden the application of functionalized carbon dots, herein, a novel dual-emission nanoprobe (FH-GA-CQDs) was prepared for ratiometric fluorescence of ClO−. FH-GA-CQDs were synthesized via the carbodiimide-activated coupling reaction with blue-emission glyoxylic acid-modified CQDs (GA-CQDs) and green-emission hydrazine hydrate-modified fluorescein (FH). For this nanoprobe, the GA was chosen as linker that connected the CQDs served as energy donor and anchoring site and the FH acted as recognition units as well as energy acceptor. The FH-GA-CQDs and its fluorescent test strips were applied for detection of ClO− in real water examples. In addition, the FH-GA-CQDs were validated for sensing of ClO− in living cells and imaging of ClO− nude mice. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first FRET probe based on fluorescein derivative-functionalized CQDs for detecting ClO−.

The CQDs with fluorescent quantum yield (φf) of 62% were prepared according to the previously reported method by our group with minor modification [29]. GA-CQDs were prepared according to the procedure reported [31]. Briefly, 0.8 g CQDs, 1 mL GA and 0.1 g CTAB were dissolved in 30 ml CH3OH/cyclohexan (1/2 mix solution). The mixture was heated to 60 °C and aged at that temperature for 6 h under nitrogen atmosphere. The solvent was removed by rotary evaporation, the resultant product was purified by silica gel column chromatography with methylene chloride/methanol (v/v, 10:1 as eluents).

The FH were synthesized by following a previous method [32]. GA-CQDs (0.3 g) and SOCl2 (10 mL) were reacted in the presence of anhydrous DMF (0.2 mL) at 80 °C for 5 h. Then, the excess SOCl2 were removed by distillation under reduced pressure. Finally, FH (0.3 g) and K2CO3 (0.3 g) were added to the above product and heated in anhydrous DMF (30 mL) at 70 °C for 30 h. The product was obtained by evaporating the above residue, then purified by silica-gel column chromatography using methylene chloride/methanol (v/v, 5:1) as eluents. The final product was dried in a vacuum at 50 ℃ overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of ClO− based on FH-GA-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The FH-GA-CQDs stock solution (2 mg mL−1) was prepared with ethanol as solvent. Initially, various analytes were mixed with 10 mL Tris buffer solution and 20 μL stock solution of FH-GA-CQDs to check the selectivity. To investigate the sensitivity towards ClO−, stock solution of FH-GA-CQDs was added into different concentrations of ClO− solution to form 10 mL clear solution. The fluorescence detection was performed in Tris buffer solution (pH 7.4) at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at excitation wavelength of 330 nm (5/5 nm slit width).

The CQDs with rich hydroxyl groups and excellent dispersion in water were firstly synthesized by following a previous method with a minor modiﬁcation [29]. As shown in Figure S1, the fluorescence spectra of CQDs exhibit red-shift with the increasing of excitation wavelength, which can attribute to different sizes of the CQDs and the distribution of different emissive sites on the surface of the CQDs [33]. The CQDs show maximum emission intensity at 398 nm (φf = 62%) when excited at 330 nm. Inspired by these behaviors, the CQDs were selected as carbon skeleton and energy donor in FH-GA-CQDs. Then, the GA was chosen as the linker, it was grafted on the surface of CQDs via acetal reaction to obtain GA-CQDs. Furthermore, the SOCl2 was used to activate the GA-CQDs to transform carboxyl group into acyl chloride, followed by amidation reaction with FH to obtain FH-GA-CQDs (Scheme 1 ).

The successful synthesis of CQDs, GA-CQDs and FH-GA-CQDs were firstly conﬁrmed by the TEM images (Fig. 1 ). As seen in Fig. 1a, d and g, the CQDs, GA-CQDs and FH-GA-CQDs possess uniform morphologies with the average diameter of 3.6 nm, 3.8 nm and 4.2 nm, respectively (Fig. 1c, f and i). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (Fig. 1b, e and h) show that the CQDs, GA-CQDs and FH-GA-CQDs have same crystallinity of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with (101) interlayer distance of graphite.27

The synthesis of FH-GA-CQDs was also confirmed by FT-IR, XPS, zeta-potential and 1H NMR. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the CQDs show the broad absorption bands at 3150 cm−1 (NH stretching vibrations) and 3450 cm-1 (OH stretching vibrations). After modified by GA, the peak at the 3450 cm-1 descend and a new absorption peak at 1750 cm-1 appear which belonged to −COOH groups. Subsequently, upon further modified with FH, the absorption peak of −COOH in GA-CQDs and -NH2 in FH disappear and the new peak at 1707 cm−1 emerge, which is attributed to γ-lactams structure. It conforms that the FH-GA-CQDs are assembly by GA-CQDs and FH. As depicted in Fig. 2b, the survey spectra of CQDs, GA-CQDs and FH-GA-CQDs display three typical peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s. GA-CQDs possess higher O content (38.1%) than that of CQDs (32.2%). Compared to GA-CQDs, the N content of the FH-GA-CQDs change from 4.2% to 6.7% and C:O atomic ratios of the FH-GA-CQDs increase from 1.5 to 2.4. The zeta potential of CQDs was measured to be −7.91 mV, which is associated to the abundant −OH on the surface of CQDs (Fig. 2c). After CQDs were modified with GA, the zeta potential of the CQDs change from −7.91 to −17.2 mV. The high-negative charges of GA-CQDs can attributed to the −COOH on GA-CQDs surface. For FH-GA-CQDs, the zeta potential decrease to −9.89 mV, which is attributed to the formation of amide bonds. Furthermore, the 1H NMR of CQDs display the peaks around 3.47 ppm and 5.27 ppm, which belong to the methylene and hydroxyl groups of the precursor of Tris (Figure S2). Compared with the CQDs, the 1H NMR of GA-CQDs don't show the peak of 5.2 ppm. It can be attributed to the happen acetal reaction leading to the consumption of hydroxyl groups. (Figure S3). Meanwhile, the resonance peak between 7.0–7.6 originate from the benzene rings are presented in FH-GA-CQDs, suggesting the successful grafting FH to GA-CQDs surface (Figure S4

To study the optical properties of FH-GA-CQDs, the UV–Vis and PL spectroscopy were carried out. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, the obvious peak is observed in spectra of CQDs at 327 nm. It can be assigned to the n−π* transitions of surface states, bringing about strong fluorescence emission [34]. while the GA-CQDs exhibit typical absorption peaks at 260 nm and 350 nm, which is ascribed to the π−π* transition of CC and the n−π* transition of CO, respectively. The FH display two absorption peak at 275 and 289 nm, respectively. In comparison, FH-GA-CQDs show absorption peaks at 275 and 287 nm and exhibit a weak shoulder peak around 360 nm which can be ascribe to the absorption of the spirocyclic form [35–38]. After the addition of ClO− into the FH-GA-CQDs solution, an apparent absorption at 492 nm from FH appear, suggesting that the spirolactam ring of the FH is opened by ClO−. When excited at 330 nm, the CQDs, GA-CQDs and FH-GA-CQDs show maximum emission wavelength at 398 nm, 418 nm and 413 nm, respectively (Fig. 3b). Compared with CQDs, the GA-CQDs have a higher degree of oxidation state, which resulted in the reducing of energy band gap of CQDs, and further led to the red shift of fluorescence emission [39]. For FH-GA-CQDs, an emission peak at 413 nm from CQDs moiety is observed, which can be attributed to changes in the surface state of the GA-CQDs [40]. Correspondingly, upon addition of ClO−, the emission peak belonging to FH appear at 508 nm, verifying that FH-GA-CQDs responded to ClO−. For an effective FRET probe, it is necessary that the spectral overlap integral between the fluorescence spectrum of energy donor and absorption spectra of the energy acceptor. As depicted in Fig. 3c, the emission band of GA-CQDs (λex = 330 nm) exhibit overlapped with the absorption band of open-loop FH units. Thus, the construction of FRET nanoprobe is theoretically possible with CQDs moiety as energy donor and FH units as energy receptor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of ClO− using FH-GA-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of FH-GA-CQDs towards ClO−, it was investigated that the fluorescence responses of FH-GA-CQDs to various analytes. As shown in Fig. 4 a and Figure S5, only ClO− result in the fluorescence emission of FH-GA-CQDs slightly decrease at 413 nm and obviously increased at 508 nm, while the other interferers show no obvious change. In addition, the UV–vis absorption spectra of FH-GA-CQDs for ROS/RNS response is shown in Fig. 4b. Compared with other ROS/RNS, the new absorption peak at 492 nm appear only upon addition of ClO−. It suggest that the ClO− promoted the opening of FH spirocyclic ring. Correspondingly, Fig. 4c present that only ClO− display clear change in visible light (colorless to green) and UV light (blue to cyan) when upon addition of 50 μM various analytes to the solution of FH-GA-CQDs. These results proved that FH-GA-CQDs possess excellent selectivity toward ClO−.

For quantitative determination of ClO− in water samples, fluorescence titration experiment for ClO− was measured. As seen from Fig. 5 a, with the concentration of ClO− increasing, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs moiety (λem =413 nm) exhibit declining tendency, whereas new emission peak from the FH units (λem =508 nm) show continuous increasing. A good linear relationship is obtained between the fluorescence intensity ratio I508/I413 with the increase of ClO− concentration from 0 to 70 μM, with a good correlation coefficient of 0.994 (inset: Fig. 5a). The limit of detection can reach as low as 93 nM based on the ratio of signal-to noise of 3. Meanwhile, the CIE color coordinates of FH-GA-CQDs vary from blue to green with the concentration of ClO− ranging from 0 to 100 μM (Fig. 5b). It is seen that the solution of FH-GA-CQDs change from colorless to green under visible light and from blue to green under UV light (Fig. 5c).

To get better detection performances, an optimal reaction time for detecting ClO− were studied. Fig. 6 a present that the solution of FH-GA-CQDs display good fluorescence stability in 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4). After addition of different concentration of ClO− to the FH-GA-CQDs solution, the ratio of I508/I413 increases dramatically and reach a plateau around 2 h. Compared with the recently reported ClO− probes, the FH-GA-CQDs need slightly longer response time, which can be associated to a smaller amount of FH grafted on GA-CQDs. The great efforts will be made in modulation the number of response groups on the surface of CQDs in the future work. Furthermore, the suitable pH range for ClO− detection were investigated in the pH range of 3.0–12.0. As seen from Fig. 6b, no obvious fluorescence change is observed with changed pH value from 3.0 to 9.0 in the absence of ClO−, which can attribute to the good pH stability of the CQDs moiety and low fluorescence emission of FH units in the state of cation, neutral and anion forms [41]. At higher pH values (> 9.0), the ratio of I508/I413 increases due to the strong fluorescence emission of the dianion form of the FH units [42]. The addition of ClO− leads to an increase in fluorescence intensity ratio over the wide pH range from 3 to 12, and the increase at these pH values are quite different. In strongly acidic solutions (pH ≤ 3.0), the presence of ClO− show little effect on the ratio of I508/I413, attributing to the low fluorescence emission of the FH units in the cationic form [43]. In strongly alkaline media (pH > 9.0), the increase of I508/I413 also result from the anionic form of the FH units [44]. Especially, the increase extent of I508/I413 has similarly high values in the pH of 5.0–9.0. Thus, the optimal conditions for detecting ClO− are reaction time 2 h and pH span 5.0–9.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of FH-GA-CQDs for ClO−
ce:section.ce:para: When added ClO− to FH-GA-CQDs solution, the spirolactam ring of FH units is oxidized to form chlorinated intermediate, and further removing HCl result in the final product with oxadiazole structure [45]. To confirm the possible mechanism, the FT-IR spectra were analyzed before and after addition of ClO−. As shown in Fig. 7 a, in the presence of ClO−, the absorption peak at 1707 cm−1 which belongs to γ-lactams structure disappeared and two new peaks at 1631 cm−1 and 1038 cm-1 appeared, which can be associated to the stretching vibration of CN bands and COC bands in oxadiazole ring.

In addition, it can be seen that the fluorescence spectrum of GA-CQDs possess overlap with the UV–vis spectrum of FH (Fig. 2c). Fig. 4a display that the fluorescence intensity gradual decrease in the emission intensity at 413 nm and concomitant increase in the emission at 508 nm with the increasing of ClO−. This phenomenon can be explained as the open-loop FH units absorbing the spectrum of CQDs moiety and happening energy transfer from CQDs moiety to FH units when FH-GA-CQDs are stimulated by ClO−. As shown in Fig. 7b, the average lifetime decreases from 12.8 ns to 8.5 ns after addition of ClO−, which provide powerful evidence that the FRET process from the CQDs moiety to FH units happened [46,47]. Furthermore, the FRET efficiency (E) and the rate of energy transfer (ke ) were estimated (Table S1). It can be seen that the R0 and r was calculated to be 12.23 Å and 13.7 Å, respectively, which is in accordance with requirements for FRET mechanism [48]. Meanwhile, the E and ke was determined to be 0.34 and 4.00 × 10−7 s-1. The detection mechanism of FH-GA-CQDs for ClO− is illustrated in Fig. 7c.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ClO− in real water with the FH-GA-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the practical applicability of FH-GA-CQDs, FH-GA-CQDs were evaluated by determination of the recovery of the spiked ClO− in tap water and pool water. As shown in Table S2, the concentrations of ClO− are found to be close to the spiked values and the recovery of ClO− varied in the range of 94%–106%, which indicate that the nanoprobe possess the ability for detection of ClO− in environmental water samples. In addition, the FH-GA-CQDs based test strips were prepared for the visual semi-quantification detection of ClO−. As shown in Fig. 8 , with the increasing of ClO−, the test strips show fluorescent gradient from blue to green. It reveals that the fluorescent test strips are promising for semi-quantification determination of ClO− in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosensing of ClO− in living cells and imaging of nude mice with the FH-GA-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the FH-GA-CQDs as fluorescent nanoprobe in biomedical applications, the cytotoxicity of the FH-GA-CQDs was conducted in VSMCs and H1299 cells by using MTT assay. As depicted in Figure S6, more than 88% of the cells remain alive with the concentration of CQDs range from 0 to 100 μg mL−1, which demonstrate that the FH-GA-CQDs are low cytotoxicity. Based on these excellent properties, the FH-GA-CQDs were applied for sensing of ClO− in VSMCs and H1299 cells by inverted fluorescence microscopy. As shown in Fig. 9 a, VSMCs emit blue fluorescence when it stained with 0.5 mg/mL of FH-GA-CQDs for 4 h (Fig. 9a). After incubation of 50 μM ClO− for 1 h, the green fluorescence is observed in the green channel (Fig. 9b). The green fluorescence signal further increases when incubation time up to 2 h (Fig. 9c). Furthermore, it can be seen that H1299 cells also display blue fluorescence in the blue channel (Fig. 9d). Fig. 9e present that the green fluorescence emission was observed when 50 μM ClO− was added and incubated for 2 h. Besides, the green further deepened in the presence of 100 μM ClO− (Fig. 9f). The above results suggest that the as-synthesized FH-GA-CQDs have excellent ability for sensing of ClO− in living cells.

The results from above cells studies endue the potential application of FH-GA-CQDs in living animal. To estimate this proposal, the imaging experiment was performed by injected FH-GA-CQDs and ClO− in abdominal cavity of nude mice. As shown in Fig. 10 , the mouse treated with 0.5 mg/mL of FH-GA-CQDs for 30 min show strong blue fluorescence under the body, suggesting the excellent water solubility of FH-GA-CQDs (Fig. 10a). Meanwhile, when the mouse was pretreated with FH-GA-CQDs and then injected with 100 μL ClO− for1 h, a significant fluorescent change is exhibited (Fig. 10b). This change is in good agreement with those of in living cells imaging of ClO− by the FH-GA-CQDs. Subsequently, a fluorescent signal converges in the bladder with the increasing of time, indicating the low toxicity of FH-GA-CQDs (Fig. 10c). These results reveal that FH-GA-CQDs possess potential capability for detecting of ClO− in living animal.

The comparison between proposed nanoprobe and some reported probes are shown in Table 1 . Even though those fluorescent probes used to detect ClO− selectively had some successful applications in biological and environmental field, most of which had drawbacks in actual applications such as poor compatibility, potential cytotoxicity, cross-sensitivity towards other ions and narrow pH detection span as well as intensity-based single emission probes. In addition, compared to ratiometric fluorescence probe of the [47] and [48], the LOD of FH-GA-CQDs is much lower than LNP/MNP and the linear range of FH-GA-CQDs is far beyond the DRFS. Thus, the nanoprobe show potential advantages for the detection of ClO−.

A novel dual-emission nanoprobe (FH-GA-CQDs) based on covalent conjugation of fluorescein derivative on the surface of carbon dots were designed and synthesized for the detection of ClO−. The FH-GA-CQDs exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity toward ClO− over other potential interferers. Before the addition of ClO−, the xanthene ring of the FH units is closed, which hinder the fluorescence emitted by CQDs can’t be absorbed by the FH units, resulting in blocking of FRET process. After the presence of ClO−, the xanthene ring is opened by oxidative induction of ClO−, the FRET process happens from CQDs to the FH units, which results in the decrease of emission peak of blue-emitting CQDs and increase of green-emitting FH units. The limit of detection for ClO− was obtained to be 93 nM. The potency of the FH-GA-CQDs had been validated by its applications in real water as fluorescent test strips for semi-quantification determination of ClO−. The FH-GA-CQDs possess the capability for resolution sensing of ClO− in living cells and imaging of ClO− in animals due to its two dual-emission falling in the “optical window”. We envision that the FH-GA-CQDs might be applied to monitor the quality of water and diagnose in physiological and pathological processes in the near future.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work described in this manuscript was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21374078, 51678409), and Tianjin Research Program of Application Foundation and Advanced Technology (No. 15JCYBJC18100, 15ZCZDSF00880), State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes (Z1-201507) and The Science and Technology Plans of Tianjin (No. 16PTSYJC00110).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.126638.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
